quizTitle: Variable Quest: What's in the Box?
description: A 6-question introduction to variables and algebra for explorers around age 11-13. It builds the idea that a variable is a named box holding a number you can change, then climbs through expressions and substitution, building any expression, solving an equation by keeping a balance level, variables that change over time, and a 'think of a number' magic trick that shows why algebra works. Every question carries a full 'Let's Learn' mini-lesson with a hands-on interactive widget and a 'now you teach it' write-and-draw prompt.

questions.question: A variable is a box that holds a number. The box x is holding 6 right now. What is x + 4?

media.title: Peek in the box
media.caption: x is holding 6. So x + 4 means 6 + 4.
media.name: x

hint.title: What Is a Variable?
hint.subtitle: The big idea: a variable is a named box that holds a number.

sections.heading: A letter that stands for a number
body: In algebra a letter like x is not a mystery code - it is just a box with a number hiding inside. The letter is the box's name, and the number it holds is its value.
Once you know what is in the box, you can use it. If the box x holds 4, then 'x + 5' simply means '4 + 5', which is 9. The letter saved us from writing the number until we were ready.

visual.title: Put a number in the box
visual.caption: Change what x holds and peek inside. Whatever the box holds, that is the value of x.
visual.name: x
visual.tip: Set x to 4, then work out x + 5 in your head. Peek to check you agree the box holds 4.

sections.heading: Why bother with a letter?
sections.body: A variable lets us talk about a number before we know it, or one that can change. 'You are x years old' works for everybody - we just put a different number in the box for each person.
points: Your age is a variable: this year it is one number, next year it is one more.
A game score is a variable: it changes every time you score.
Money in your pocket is a variable: it goes up and down through the day.

sections.heading: Now you teach it: write it and draw it
sections.body: Lock the idea in by explaining it yourself.
points: Write one sentence: 'A variable is ...' in your own words.
Draw a box, label it y, and draw 7 dots inside. Then write 'y = 7' next to it.
Tell someone what 'y + 2' would be when your box holds 7.

questions.question: Every x is the SAME box, so it holds the same number everywhere. If x = 5, what is x + x + x?

media.title: Three boxes, one value
media.caption: All three x boxes hold the same number, because x is one box.
media.name: x

hint.title: Same Name, Same Number
hint.subtitle: Why x + x + x is the same as 3x.

sections.heading: One box, used three times
body: If you see x more than once in the same problem, every x is the identical box holding the identical number. Change it in one place and it changes in all of them.
So x + x + x is three copies of the same number. We write that short as 3x, which means '3 lots of x'. If x = 2, then x + x + x = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6, and 3x = 3 x 2 = 6 too.

visual.title: Build 3 boxes of x
visual.caption: Set 3 boxes and 0 loose ones. Slide x and watch all three boxes fill the same.
visual.tip: Slide x. Notice every box always holds the same amount - that is what 'same variable' means.

sections.heading: A real shorthand
sections.body: Writing 3x instead of x + x + x is like writing 'three coffees' instead of 'a coffee and a coffee and a coffee'. Mathematicians are happily lazy - the short form means exactly the same thing.

sections.heading: Now you teach it: write it and draw it
points: Draw three identical boxes labelled n, each holding 4 dots. Write 'n + n + n = 3n = 12'.
Write in words why all three boxes must hold the same number.
Try x = 10 in your head: what is x + x + x, and what is 3x?

questions.question: A function machine has the rule: multiply by 3, then add 1. You drop in the number 4. What comes out?

media.title: Run the machine
media.caption: Drop a number in, the rule changes it, the answer drops out.

hint.title: The Function Machine
hint.subtitle: A rule that turns an input into an output.

sections.heading: Numbers go in, numbers come out
body: A function machine takes a number in, does the same rule to it every time, and sends a new number out. The input is a variable - you can put in anything you like.
If the rule is 'multiply by 3, then add 1', the machine is really doing 3 x (your number) + 1. Put in 2 and you get 7. Put in 5 and you get 16. The rule never changes - only the number you feed it.

visual.title: Set the rule, feed it numbers
visual.caption: Set the rule, type a number, and press Run. Watch the in/out table fill up.
visual.tip: Feed it 1, 2 then 3 and watch the outputs. Can you predict the next one before pressing Run?

sections.heading: Machines are everywhere
sections.body: A vending machine takes coins in and gives a snack out. A 'double your money' deal is a machine with the rule x times 2. A taxi fare is a machine: a fixed charge plus a rule for each mile.
sections.note: Detective mode: in 'Guess my rule', you only see the inputs and outputs. Spotting the hidden rule is exactly what scientists do with data.

sections.heading: Now you teach it: write it and draw it
points: Draw a machine box. Write your own rule on it, like 'x 2 + 5'.
Make an in/out table for the inputs 1, 2, 3 using your rule.
Challenge someone: give them three in/out pairs and see if they can guess your rule.

questions.question: An equation is a balance that must stay level. Solve x + 3 = 8. What is x?

media.title: Balance the scale
media.caption: Slide what is in the box until both pans weigh the same.

hint.title: Solving by Balancing
hint.subtitle: Keep both sides equal and the unknown reveals itself.

sections.heading: An equals sign is a balance point
body: The equals sign in x + 3 = 8 means the two sides weigh exactly the same. The box x is a hidden weight, and we want to know how heavy it is.

The golden rule: whatever you do to one side, do to the other, and it stays balanced. Take 3 away from both sides of x + 3 = 8 and you are left with x = 5. The scale never tipped, so the answer is safe.

visual.title: Try a different one
visual.caption: Here is x + 2 = 6. Slide the box until it balances to find x.
visual.tip: Slide slowly. When the beam is level, the number in the box is your answer.

sections.heading: Where balancing shows up
sections.body: Sharing a bill fairly, balancing a seesaw, mixing a recipe so both batches match - any time two things must come out equal, you are really solving an equation.

sections.heading: Now you teach it: write it and draw it
points: Draw a balance. Put a box and 2 weights on the left, 6 weights on the right.
Cross off 2 weights from BOTH sides and write what is left.
Write the answer: 'the box weighs ___', then check it by putting it back in x + 2 = 6.

questions.question: You have $5 and save $2 every day. Using the variable d for days, how much will you have after 4 days?

media.title: Watch your savings grow
media.caption: Start at $5 and add $2 each day. Step to day 4.

hint.title: Variables That Change
hint.subtitle: A box whose value grows step by step.

sections.heading: A variable can change over time
body: Some variables hold still, and some change. Your savings change every day: they start somewhere and grow by a fixed amount each time.

We can capture the whole pattern in one rule: total = start + (amount each day) x (number of days). With $5 to start and $3 a day, after d days you have 5 + 3d. That single rule works for day 1, day 2, or day 100.

visual.title: Step through the days
visual.caption: This jar starts at $3 and adds $3 a day. Step forward and read the rule as it updates.
visual.tip: Notice the rule x = start + step x days. Predict day 5 before you slide to it.

sections.heading: Changing variables are everywhere
sections.body: A game score climbs as you play, a phone battery falls each hour, your height grows each year. Each is a variable with a starting value and a rule for how it changes.

sections.heading: Now you teach it: write it and draw it
points: Write the rule for $5 start and $2 a day: 'total = 5 + 2d'.
Make a small table: day 1, 2, 3, 4 and the total for each.
Invent your own: pick a start and a daily amount, and write its rule.

questions.question: Think of any number. Double it, add 10, halve the result, then subtract your starting number. What do you ALWAYS get?

media.title: The mind-reading trick
media.caption: Pick a secret number and step through - the answer is always the same.

hint.title: Why the Magic Always Works
hint.subtitle: Let x be any number and watch it cancel out.

sections.heading: Let x be your secret number
body: The trick feels like mind-reading, but it is pure algebra. Instead of testing every number, we let one box, x, stand for ALL of them at once.

Follow the steps in algebra: start with x, double it to get 2x, add 10 to get 2x + 10, halve it to get x + 5, then subtract your number x. The x's cancel and you are left with just 5 - no matter what you started with.

visual.title: Run both tracks together
visual.caption: Step through and watch your number and the secret x side by side. See where the x disappears.
visual.tip: Try a few different secret numbers. The 'your number' column changes every time, but the last row is always 5.

sections.heading: This is the superpower of variables
sections.body: One letter let us prove something about every number in the universe in four short lines. That is why algebra is so powerful: a variable says 'this works for anything'.

sections.heading: Now you teach it: write it and draw it
points: Write the four steps next to the algebra: x, 2x, 2x + 10, x + 5, then 5.
Circle the moment the x cancels when you subtract your number.
Perform the trick on a family member, then show them why it works.
